did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (MobileNet, Xception, ResNet50, ResNet50V2, DenseNet121) via transfer learning and an ensemble stacking of machine learning models (logistic regression, SVM, random forest, KNN, gradient boosting machine) trained on hand-crafted features to classify malignant and benign skin lesions from the ISIC 2018 dataset for melanoma diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of deep learning and ensemble stacking machine learning models for melanoma diagnosis. It utilizes the ISIC 2018 dataset, describes specific methodologies (transfer learning, feature extraction, stacking), reports quantitative results (accuracy, F1-score, p-values), and compares its performance against existing methods, which characterizes it as a primary research study rather than a review.

title: A Non-Invasive Interpretable Diagnosis of Melanoma Skin Cancer Using Deep Learning and Ensemble Stacking of Machine Learning Models
brief_summary: This study presents a non-invasive, interpretable method for diagnosing melanoma skin cancer using deep learning and ensemble stacking of machine learning models. The researchers utilized the ISIC 2018 dataset, specifically a balanced two-class version containing malignant and benign skin lesions. The methodology involved training base machine learning models (logistic regression, SVM, random forest, KNN, and gradient boosting) on hand-crafted features and ensembling deep learning models (MobileNet, Xception, ResNet50, ResNet50V2, and DenseNet121) via transfer learning. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, Cohen’s kappa, confusion matrices, and ROC curves, with statistical significance assessed via the corrected paired Student’s t-test. The proposed approach achieved a final accuracy of 92.0% and an AUC score of 97.0%, outperforming several existing methods in the comparative analysis. Additionally, SHAP-based interpretability was employed to generate heatmaps, allowing dermatologists to visualize the image regions most suggestive of malignancy.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis. The study uses a balanced two-class subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset containing malignant skin lesions (melanoma) and benign skin lesions. The paper focuses on non-invasive diagnosis of melanoma.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. The study uses a balanced two-class subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset containing benign skin lesions to contrast with malignant melanoma. While the full ISIC 2018 dataset has seven classes, this experiment specifically uses a binary malignant vs. benign split.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant (melanoma) and benign categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification using a balanced two-class subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset containing malignant and benign skin lesions. The models predict discrete diagnostic labels (malignant vs. benign) using deep learning (MobileNet, Xception, ResNet50, ResNet50V2, DenseNet121) and ensemble stacking of machine learning models (logistic regression, SVM, random forest, KNN, gradient boosting). Evaluation metrics include accuracy, F1-score, Cohen’s kappa, confusion matrices, and ROC-AUC, confirming a classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset (Miniature/Balanced Subset)
datasets_used.description: A curated, balanced two-class subset of the original ISIC 2018 challenge dataset. The original dataset contains seven classes, but this study excluded all disease categories except melanoma and selected an equal number of benign and malignant images to create a balanced binary classification dataset. Images are dermoscopic skin lesions with dimensions of 224x224 pixels.

use.training: Used to train base machine learning models (logistic regression, SVM, random forest, KNN, gradient boosting) on hand-crafted features and to fine-tune deep learning models (MobileNet, Xception, ResNet50, ResNet50V2, DenseNet121) via transfer learning. Data augmentation was applied to the training set.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the trained models using metrics such as accuracy, F1-score, Cohen’s kappa, confusion matrices, and ROC curves.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine whether the differences in performance between the proposed ensembling model and base CNN models were statistically significant.
To reject the null hypothesis (no difference in performance) if the p-value was below 5%, confirming significant performance gains.
To select images from the ISIC 2018 dataset, ensuring proportional allocation of samples for each stratum (benign and malignant classes).
Used on the training set to train the level one model stacking of machine learning models.
To measure the agreement between true labels and predictions, serving as a prominent quantitative metric for model effectiveness.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: No difference in performance exists between the two deep learning models when comparing them.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The two deep learning models exhibit significant differences in performance.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined by p-values less than 0.05, indicating that the differences in performances of the studied models were statistically significant.

method_1_machine_learning.description: Base models trained on hand-crafted features (Hu moments, Haralick, color histogram) with level-one stacking.

method_2_deep_learning.description: Transfer learning models fine-tuned on ImageNet pre-trained weights, followed by ensembling.

ISIC 2018 (Balanced Two-Class Subset).notes: The best performing model was the Ensembling of the 3 best deep learning models, achieving 92.0% accuracy, 92.0% recall (sensitivity), 91.0% precision, 0.83 Cohen's kappa, and 0.97 AUC. Statistical significance was assessed using the corrected paired Student’s t-test with p < 0.05.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of Deep Learning models (MobileNet, Xception, ResNet50, ResNet50V2, DenseNet121) and Ensemble Stacking of Machine Learning models (Logistic Regression, SVM, Random Forest, KNN, Gradient Boosting Machine)
architecture_used.pretraining: Deep learning backbones were pre-trained on ImageNet data.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Hand-crafted features including Hu moments, Haralick features (GLCM), and color histogram for ML models; automated features via CNN for DL models.
architecture_details.ensemble_method: Level one stacking for ML models using 5-fold cross-validation; Ensembling of best DL models (combinations of 3, 4, or 5 models).
architecture_details.modifications: Top fully connected layer of pre-trained DL models was removed; Global Average Pooling (GAP) was attached; new classification head with softmax activation was added.

training_details.learning_rate: Initial rate of 0.001 with a decay of 1 × 10^-6
training_details.training_strategy: Transfer learning with fine-tuning. First, backbone weights were frozen to train the classification head. Then, all layers were trained to fine-tune the models.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with Softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of input image data to a range between -1 and +1
Data augmentation on the training set including rotation (range 90), shear (range 0.1), zoom (range 0.1), horizontal flip, vertical flip, and shuffling

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a rotation range of 90 degrees.
Applied with a shear range of 0.1.
Applied with a zoom range of 0.1.

conclusions: The proposed method of ensemble stacking of machine learning models and deep learning ensembling provides a non-invasive and interpretable diagnosis for melanoma skin cancer.

The ensemble of the three best deep learning models (ResNet50, ResNet50V2, and DenseNet121) achieved the highest performance with 92.0% accuracy, 91.0% precision, 92.0% sensitivity (recall), 0.83 Cohen’s kappa, and 0.97 AUC, outperforming all individual base models and the machine learning stacking approach.
Deep learning models generally demonstrated higher accuracy (mostly >90%) compared to machine learning models (all <90%) on the balanced ISIC 2018 dataset.
Level one stacking of machine learning models improved performance over individual base models, achieving 88.0% accuracy, but remained inferior to the deep learning ensemble.

The use of SHAP-based interpretability successfully generated heatmaps that identified image regions most suggestive of malignancy, allowing dermatologists to understand the model's decision-making process.
The study addresses challenges of image occlusions and imbalanced datasets by using a balanced two-class subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset and data augmentation techniques.
The proposed approach outperforms several existing methods in comparative analysis, specifically matching or exceeding the accuracy of other state-of-the-art techniques reported in the literature.
important_remarks: The study uses a curated, balanced two-class subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset (excluding all disease categories except melanoma and benign) rather than the full multi-class dataset, which limits the generalizability to other skin lesion types.
The dataset is small (3,297 total images) and derived from a single public challenge, raising concerns about external validation and potential population bias.
The paper acknowledges that while the model aids dermatologists, it is not intended to replace their knowledge and cannot solely rely on skin lesion images to advise a patient’s treatment plan.
The comparative analysis against existing methods is limited because many cited studies used different datasets (e.g., PH2, ISBI 2017, DermNet), making direct performance comparisons difficult.

The study addresses the 'black-box' nature of deep learning by incorporating SHAP-based interpretability to generate heatmaps, aiming to improve clinical acceptance by identifying regions suggestive of malignancy.
The models were trained on dermoscopic images, but the paper notes that image occlusions (e.g., hair, shading) and imbalanced datasets are common challenges in the field that can compromise accuracy.
